summary: Fowler reflects on his early experience with the iPad, explaining how it fits his lifestyle of reading and browsing away from his desk better than a laptop. He praises the form factor, battery life, screen quality, and touch interface while noting concerns about ebook lock-in, web app limitations, and wifi restrictions. Despite not considering himself an Apple fanboy, he compares the sensation of using the iPad to first experiencing the World Wide Web in 1995, suggesting it represents a paradigm shift in how we consume content.
key_points: The iPad's form factor and battery life solve the awkwardness of using laptops for casual reading and browsing
Ebook platform lock-in is a significant concern, with Amazon's Kindle potentially being more portable than iBooks
Web apps designed with hover interactions don't translate well to touch interfaces
Apple's curated software approach has both drawbacks and benefits compared to open systems
The iPad represents a potential paradigm shift similar to the emergence of the World Wide Web
money_quotes: After a few hours with the iPad, I realized that I was feeling a sensation I'd only remembered once before: back in 1995 when someone showed me the World Wide Web.
There's no dramatic new technology, we've seen tablets before just as we'd seen hypertext before. But the overall package was game-changer.
I don't know if the iPad will be The Device, but I do think that this kind of device will make huge difference to how we read and watch things in the future.
Given how much of my life I've lost to sorting out software incompatibilities I'm not sure whether to like curation or not.
I certainly need to work at making sure what I produce in the future is built with these kinds of devices in mind.
key_insight: Transformative technology products aren't necessarily built on dramatic new inventions, but rather on combining existing technologies into a package that fundamentally changes user behavior.
